summary:
The Samara culture was an Eneolithic culture of the Volga-Ural steppe region in present-day Russia, dating to approximately 5000–4500 BCE and centered on the Samara River valley. It is one of the earliest cultures in the region with a clearly pastoral economy emphasizing cattle herding and possibly early horse use. The Samara culture is considered a precursor to the [[event:1542|Khvalynsk culture]] and part of the broader cultural sequence leading to the steppe cultures associated with the [[event:1544|Proto-Indo-European homeland]].

Samara communities occupied a transitional zone between the forest zone to the north and the open steppe to the south, herding cattle and possibly sheep while maintaining some hunting and foraging. The famous Samara cemetery, excavated beginning in 1973, provided the type assemblage for the culture and yielded skeletal remains that have been subject to ancient DNA analysis — the results of which have been foundational in the ongoing scientific debate about Indo-European origins and the genetic history of European populations.

The Samara culture represents an early stage in the development of the steppe pastoralist tradition that would ultimately generate one of the most consequential events in prehistoric human history: the expansion of Yamnaya-related populations into Europe and South Asia carrying Proto-Indo-European languages. As such, it occupies a significant position in narratives of Eurasian prehistory despite its modest material culture.
